topic: An employee is transferred to a different department
raw_output:
[story 1]

The email landed in Sarah’s inbox with the blunt finality of a gavel strike. Transfer to the Marketing division. Sarah stared at the screen, her breath catching in her throat. She had spent three years cultivating a niche expertise in data analysis, a world away from the vibrant, chaotic realm of social media campaigns. A slow, cold dread began to pool in her stomach. When her manager called her in the next day, the polite smile she usually wore felt brittle, stretched thin over a core of sheer panic. "It's an exciting opportunity, Sarah," he chirped, oblivious to the tremor in her hands as she clutched her lukewarm coffee mug. She could only manage a strained, "Right. Of course." Every step toward the elevator felt heavy, each floor a descent into unfamiliar territory.

[story 2]

Mark kept running his thumb over the worn edge of his desk blotter. The official memo detailing his relocation to the Operations team felt like an accusation. He kept replaying the meeting—the way his supervisor had leaned back, a gesture that suddenly seemed patronizing, and the way the other department heads had exchanged knowing glances. He felt a flush creep up his neck, hot and undeniable. When he finally approached the HR representative, his voice came out unnaturally high, a reedy sound he couldn't quite control. "So, I'm moving over to logistics?" he managed, forcing a laugh that sounded hollow even to his own ears. He desperately wished the floor would swallow him whole, anywhere but the brightly lit hallway where he knew he’d have to face the inevitable awkwardness.

[story 3]

Elena spent the entire morning meticulously re-reading the transfer documentation, underlining every bureaucratic phrase as if it held the key to escaping the situation. The thought of navigating the entirely different corporate culture of the Product Development unit made her palms slick. During lunch, she deliberately avoided the usual gathering spot, opting instead for a quiet corner of the breakroom, where the hum of the refrigerator seemed deafening. When her colleague from the new department, Ben, approached her with an overly enthusiastic greeting, Elena simply nodded, her gaze fixed firmly on a water stain on the ceiling tiles. She felt a distinct urge to shrink, to become invisible, wishing the floor would simply open up and let her fall through.
